title: Vector Databases
lesson:
A vector database is a special kind of storage built to answer one question fast: what do I have that means something similar to this? Not "find the exact word" but "find the closest idea". To get there, we first need to remember what a vector is.

From meaning to numbers

An Embeddings model turns a piece of text (or an image, or audio) into a long list of numbers, say 768 or 1536 of them. That list is the vector. The clever part: texts with similar meaning get vectors that sit close together in this number-space, and unrelated texts sit far apart. "Refund my order" and "I want my money back" land near each other; "the weather in Paris" lands far away.

Think of it like a giant map of a city. Every sentence is a pin. Restaurants cluster in one neighbourhood, hardware shops in another. A vector database is the system that holds millions of these pins and, when you drop a new pin, instantly tells you the nearest neighbours.

Why not just use a normal database?

A normal database is a phone book. It is brilliant at exact matches: "give me the row where id = 42" or "rows where city = 'Paris'". But it has no idea that "sofa" and "couch" are the same thing.

A vector database is built for Semantic Search instead, search by meaning. You hand it a query vector and ask: "give me the 5 stored vectors closest to this one." That closeness is measured by distance, usually:

  • Cosine similarity — how much two arrows point the same direction.
  • Euclidean distance — the straight-line gap between two points.

The speed trick: ANN

Here is the problem. If you have 10 million stored vectors and check the distance to every single one for each query, you do 10 million math operations per search. That is too slow.

So vector databases cheat, intelligently. They use ANN (Approximate Nearest Neighbour) search: instead of checking everyone, they check a smart shortlist and accept answers that are almost always right.

The popular method is HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World). Picture getting from London to a small town in Japan. You do not drive the whole way. You take a long-haul flight to Tokyo (the top layer, big jumps), then a train to the region (middle layer), then a taxi to the door (bottom layer, tiny steps). HNSW builds these layers of shortcuts so a search hops quickly to the right neighbourhood, then refines, finishing in a few milliseconds even over millions of vectors.

Metadata and filtering

Each vector is stored with a payload, extra fields like the original text, a document ID, a date, or a user ID. This lets you mix meaning-search with normal filters: "find chunks similar to this question, but only from documents this user owns, written after January." This filtering is what makes vector databases usable in real products, not toys.

A tiny example

import chromadb
client = chromadb.Client()
col = client.create_collection("faqs")

# store text + auto-embed it (Chroma embeds for you)
col.add(ids=["1","2"],
        documents=["How do I reset my password?",
                   "What are your opening hours?"],
        metadatas=[{"topic":"account"},{"topic":"info"}])

# search by meaning
hits = col.query(query_texts=["I forgot my login"], n_results=1)
print(hits["documents"])  # -> password reset FAQ

Notice we searched with "I forgot my login" and got back the password FAQ, even though no words matched. That is the whole point.

Where it fits

Vector databases are the memory layer behind RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation). The flow: split documents into pieces (see Chunking), embed each piece, store them, then at question-time embed the question, fetch the closest chunks, and paste them into the model's prompt. The same machinery powers AI Memory Systems that let an assistant recall past conversations.

You have choices: hosted services like Pinecone, or open-source engines like Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus, and Chroma. For small projects, pgvector bolts vector search onto plain PostgreSQL, no new database needed. As of 2026, most teams start with pgvector or Chroma and only graduate to a dedicated engine when they cross tens of millions of vectors.

💡 The vector database does not understand language. It only does fast geometry. All the "meaning" lives in the embedding vectors you feed it. Garbage vectors in, garbage neighbours out.
takeaways: Stores embeddings and finds the closest ones by meaning, not exact words.
ANN (e.g. HNSW) returns near-perfect matches in milliseconds across millions.
Match your embedding model + distance metric, and store metadata for filtering.

diagram.caption: A query is embedded into a vector, then the database finds the geometrically closest stored vectors (its nearest neighbours) and returns the top matches.

quiz.q: You search for "end my subscription" but the matching FAQ says "How do I cancel my plan?" — no words overlap. Why does a vector database still find it?
options: It uses fuzzy spell-checking to repair the words
Both texts embed to vectors that point in nearly the same direction
It scans every row and matches synonyms from a built-in dictionary
It stores the FAQ under a 'cancel' keyword tag it matched
quiz.explain: Similar meanings produce vectors that sit close together, so the query vector lands near the FAQ vector even with zero shared words. No dictionary or keyword match is involved.

quiz.q: A search over 8 million vectors returns in a few milliseconds because the database uses ANN (e.g. HNSW). What is the trade-off?
options: It uses far more disk but is always exact
It may occasionally miss the true closest item
It only works for fewer than 1,000 vectors
It requires the query and stored vectors to be identical
quiz.explain: ANN means Approximate Nearest Neighbour: it checks a smart shortlist instead of everything, trading a sliver of accuracy (recall) for huge speed. It is almost always right but not guaranteed exact.

quiz.q: Your retrieval quality is fine, but each user can sometimes see other users' private chunks. What was most likely skipped?
options: Choosing cosine instead of Euclidean distance
Storing and filtering on a user-ID in each vector's metadata
Switching from HNSW to brute-force search
quiz.explain: Vectors carry a payload of metadata (user ID, date, doc ID). Without storing and filtering on a user ID, the search returns the closest chunks regardless of who owns them.

quiz.q: You stored your documents using embedding model A, then started embedding queries with model B to save cost. Results got nonsensical. Why?
options: Model B produces longer text, breaking the index
Vectors from different models live in different spaces, so distances are meaningless
The HNSW graph can only hold one model's vectors at a time by license
Model B requires Euclidean distance and the index uses cosine
quiz.explain: Each model defines its own coordinate system, so a query vector from model B is not comparable to stored vectors from model A. Always embed stored data and queries with the same model and version.

mentalModel: A vector database is a librarian who shelves books by idea instead of by title, then, when you describe a vague feeling, instantly hands you the few books that are closest to it.
mistakes: Mixing embedding models. Vectors made by model A are not comparable to vectors made by model B. If you re-embed your query with a different model than your stored data, distances become meaningless. Always use the same model (and version) for storing and searching.

Trusting ANN to be exact. Approximate Nearest Neighbour search trades a tiny bit of accuracy for huge speed. It can occasionally miss the true closest item. For most apps that is fine, but do not assume it is perfect, and tune the recall setting if accuracy matters.

Forgetting metadata filters. Without storing a user ID or document ID alongside each vector, you cannot scope results, so one user can retrieve another user's private chunks. Store and filter on metadata from day one.

Wrong distance metric. Many embedding models expect cosine similarity. If your index is set to Euclidean (or you forget to normalise vectors), rankings come out subtly wrong and you blame the model instead of the config.

Treating it as your only database. A vector database is bad at exact lookups, transactions, and joins. Keep your normal database for source-of-truth data; use the vector store as a search index beside it.
Putting in huge or messy chunks. Embedding a whole 50-page PDF as one vector blurs its meaning into mush. Smaller, clean chunks give sharper, more useful neighbours.
exercise:
Build a 10-line semantic search over a tiny FAQ. Pick any local vector store (Chroma is easiest, or pgvector if you have Postgres). Store 5 FAQ questions like "How do I cancel my subscription?" and "Do you ship to Canada?". Then query with paraphrases that share no exact words, e.g. "end my plan" and "is delivery available abroad". Confirm the right FAQ comes back top.

Stretch goal: Add a category field to each FAQ's metadata, then run the same query but filtered to only one category. Watch how filtering changes (and sometimes worsens) the results, and write one sentence on why.
solution: In Chroma, collection.add(ids=..., documents=..., metadatas=[{"category":"billing"}, ...]) stores and auto-embeds. Then collection.query(query_texts=["end my plan"], n_results=1) returns the cancel-subscription FAQ because their embedding vectors point in nearly the same direction, no shared words required. For the stretch goal, pass where={"category": "shipping"} to query. If your best semantic match lives in a different category, filtering drops it and you get a worse answer, that is the classic tension between precise filtering and recall (finding everything relevant).
summary: A vector database stores embeddings (lists of numbers that capture meaning) and finds the closest ones to a query fast, using Approximate Nearest Neighbour tricks like HNSW. It searches by meaning, not exact words, stores metadata for filtering, and is the memory engine behind RAG and AI memory. Match your embedding model and distance metric, store metadata early, and keep your normal database for exact data.

keyTerms.def: A long list of numbers (e.g. 1536 of them) that represents the meaning of a piece of text, image, or audio. Similar meanings produce vectors that sit close together.
Storage built to find the stored vectors most similar to a query vector, quickly, even across millions of items.
Finding the stored items closest to your query in number-space, i.e. the most similar in meaning.
A search that returns answers that are almost always the true closest, trading a sliver of accuracy for big speed gains.
Hierarchical Navigable Small World, a layered index of shortcuts (like long-haul flight, then train, then taxi) that lets ANN search hop to the right region in milliseconds.
A way to measure how alike two vectors are by how much they point in the same direction; common for text embeddings.
The straight-line gap between two points in number-space; another way to measure how far apart two vectors are.
Extra fields stored with each vector (text, user ID, date, category) used to filter search results.
The share of truly relevant items a search actually finds. Higher recall means fewer misses, often at the cost of speed.
An extension that adds vector search to ordinary PostgreSQL, letting you avoid running a separate vector database for small to medium workloads.
